text: Hello, my name is Alex Smith. I am a cardiothoracic registrar, currently based at Guy's Hospital in London. And I am presenting today a video of robotic assisted diaphragmatic repair and pleurectomy undertaken by Mr. Andrea Bile.

The case was of a forty-year-old lady who had initially presented with cyclical abdominal pain having been diagnosed with endometriosis. A pelvic MRI scan and CT scan of the chest revealed a twenty-five millimetre soft tissue nodule in the area of the right diaphragm. The patient was therefore consented for robotic assisted resection of endometrial body as well as a pleurectomy to prevent the possible occurrence of catamenal pneumothorax.

The surgical team uses three robotic ports and an assistant port, with fenestrated bipolar forceps, a camera, and a permanent cautery spatula positioned to face downwards for optimal visualization of the diaphragm. The diaphragm is inspected, and visible endometrial tissue deposits are removed, including a prominent nodule. The overview reveals marked endometrial deposits and a large fenestrated area with the liver visible beneath.

The surgeon uses bipolar cautery and a spatula to resect abnormal nodular tissue on the diaphragm. The bipolar forceps retract the tissue while the assistant holds a suction catheter visible on the left side of the image, enhancing visibility in the surgical field.

The surgeon completes the resection of the diaphragm using a spatula to gently dissect away from adhesions near the liver. The procedure transitions to a pleurectomy in the costophrenic recess, facilitated by the robotic pelvic position for enhanced access.

The surgeon repairs the diaphragm using a layered Ethibond stitch technique, starting with a continuous running stitch and reinforcing with multiple interrupted stitches. The robotic instruments facilitate precise stitching, and the final view shows the diaphragm with small resected endometrial deposits.

The surgeon introduces a Teflon patch to complete the diaphragm repair, loosely stitching it in place with multiple interrupted stitches. The final appearance of the diaphragm is shown. The robot is undocked and redocked through the same ports for a thoracic approach, with the camera view shifting towards the apex to begin pleurectomy on the anterior chest wall.

The robotic system provides enhanced visualization and precision as the surgeon performs an extensive pleurectomy across the chest wall. A small patch is visible on the anterior chest wall, which is removed before inserting a single chest drain and inflating the lung prior to closure.
So that's the end of our video for today. I hope you found it interesting and thank you very much.
